author: @samuelramesh8621
text:
@Robert Murray-Smith,
My take on this Reluctance Generator (RG) is that, you need to spin the rotor to high RMP 2000 RMP to get considerable amount of power. Also I feel that when loaded the RG will experience less back drag, because the magnetic field due to load current will cancel with the magnetic field of permanent magnet, as a there will be less back drag on the rotor, This could be the best candidate for Overunity, but still there will be energy loss due to friction, windage and heat.

replies.author: @williambianchi2006
replies.text:
Whenever I see a generator that is mentioned with the term overunity, it makes me think that the generator is possibly more efficient than standard generators---less input for the same or better output as compared to a standard comparable generator.
For instance, could this generator allow a human powered setup, bicycle or hand cranked, to generate more power than a standard human powered generator or alternator? How about a stirling heat motor, could this generator get one to output a usable amount of electricity for battery charging?

replies.author: @Chris-uu2td
replies.text:
I don't think that higher load will decrease the counter torque the generator is producing.
I mean, you are right, that the coils fields and the permanent magnets field will superimpose. But (according to the induction law) in order to induce voltage and thereby produce electrical current, the magnetic flux inside the coils has to change.
The stronger the current in the coils, the stroner their magentic field will be, leading to the temporary formation of two "super" poles diametrically opposed to each other. The magnetic reluctance will pull the rotor in line with these super poles. So in order to switch the magnetic flux through the coils and to move away from these "super" poles, the rotor has to be driven with higher torgue, the stronger the current through the coils is.

replies.author: @bugz000
replies.text:
@Kiyarose3999 it is inductance motor, so i think like an alternator it needs an induction current, as in you must apply power to it, for it to generate power - it will generate it's own starting power using those magnets but by applying power to the/a coil, the other will pick up far higher flux combined with the magnets.... so it is my understanding you can increase the resistance and therefore the ideal RPM of this generator by changing the applied current to the coil.... (i may be entirely wrong mind!!)
it seems "lossy" to have to apply current to make power but it is less lossy than a gearbox!  as i understand it is effectively like running a variable CVT with no extra mechanical parts

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry mate - it is not an inductance motor - it is switched reluctance - you. might want to have a bit of a read?

author: @paulmaydaynight9925
text:
hmm, so laminated pressed/sheered steel shapes, stacked laser cut kitchen foil, & steel/copper wire will work, put through an auto desktop esp32 wire bender... straight horizontal wire bend.. down, up, right, down, up, right etc
wind -reclaimed, not destroyed expensive precision- insulated copper as you go, -or just use my prior cored wound copper description for simplicity & time as the base material, or conductive painted then insulated long paper 1-2mm strips for a fun first real life poc paper generator- then shape into a half circle.. the outer down/up part will separate making it simple, stack the wires as many as you like to fit a bean can etc, rinse repeat for the rotor
